label: Buenos Aires
sub: Federal capital; center of Argentine political, economic, and cultural life. Permanently refounded by Juan de Garay in 1580. Population: ~3 million city, ~15 million metro.

label: Casa Rosada
sub: Presidential palace on Plaza de Mayo. Site of Perón's balcony addresses, De la Rúa's helicopter escape in 2001, and Milei's inauguration in 2023.

label: Plaza de Mayo
sub: Historic center of Argentine political life. Scene of May Revolution (1810), October 17 Peronist uprising (1945), Madres de Plaza de Mayo marches (1977–present), and 2001 protests.

label: ESMA — Memory Museum
sub: Escuela Superior de Mecánica de la Armada. Argentina's most notorious clandestine detention center during the Dirty War (1976–1983). ~5,000 prisoners passed through; fewer than 200 survived. Now a UNESCO Memory and Human Rights Museum.

label: AMIA Bombing Site
sub: On July 18, 1994, a car bomb destroyed the AMIA Jewish community center at Pasteur 633, Buenos Aires. 85 killed, 300+ injured. Deadliest terrorist attack in Argentine history; perpetrators never prosecuted.

label: Campo de Mayo
sub: Argentina's largest military base, northwest of Buenos Aires. Served as a major clandestine detention and extermination center during the 1976–1983 Dirty War. Site of 'Pozo de Banfield' and numerous disappearances.

label: Córdoba
sub: Argentina's second city. Founded 1573. Site of the Cordobazo (1969) workers' uprising against the Onganía dictatorship. Home of La Perla clandestine detention center. Major industrial and university hub.

label: La Perla — Detention Site
sub: La Perla, near Córdoba, was the largest clandestine detention center in Argentina's interior. An estimated 2,200 people were held and tortured here during the 1976–1983 Dirty War; most were killed. Now a memorial museum.

label: Tucumán — Casa Histórica
sub: San Miguel de Tucumán: site of the Declaration of Independence on July 9, 1816. The Casa Histórica de la Independencia is Argentina's most sacred historical monument. City is also Argentina's sugar industry center.

label: Mendoza
sub: Base of San Martín's Army of the Andes (1816–1817) for the liberation of Chile and Peru. Today Argentina's wine capital and gateway to Aconcagua (6,961m, highest peak in the Americas).

label: Rosario
sub: Birthplace of the Argentine national flag, raised by Manuel Belgrano on February 27, 1812. Also birthplace of Ernesto 'Che' Guevara (June 14, 1928). Argentina's third city and major Paraná river port.

label: Santa Fe — Constitutional Congress
sub: Site of the Constitutional Congress that adopted Argentina's 1853 federal constitution on May 1, 1853. The constitution established the federal republic and opened the country to mass European immigration.

label: Islas Malvinas / Falkland Islands
sub: Disputed territory occupied by Britain since 1833. Argentina invaded on April 2, 1982; British forces retook the islands by June 14. Argentina lost 649 troops; Britain 255. Argentine sovereignty claim remains active. UK calls them Falkland Islands.

label: Puerto Argentino (Stanley)
sub: Capital of the Falkland Islands (UK) / Puerto Argentino (Argentina). Argentine forces surrendered to British General Moore here on June 14, 1982, ending the 74-day Malvinas War.

label: Vaca Muerta
sub: The world's second-largest shale gas and fourth-largest shale oil formation. Located in Neuquén Province. Discovered 2010; YPF began large-scale development post-2012 nationalization. Producing ~450 kb/d oil equivalent by 2024; Argentina's key export asset.

label: Neuquén
sub: Provincial capital of Neuquén; gateway to Vaca Muerta shale fields. Rapidly growing oil and gas industry hub. Site of major YPF, Tecpetrol, TotalEnergies, and Shell operations.

label: Ushuaia
sub: Southernmost city in the world. Capital of Tierra del Fuego Province. Site of the infamous Ushuaia Penal Colony (1884–1947) where anarchists and criminals were exiled. Strategic during Malvinas War as Argentine naval base.

label: Salta
sub: Founded 1582. Important colonial and independence-era city. Site of General Belgrano's victory at Battle of Salta (1813). Gateway to Andean northwestern Argentina (NOA region) and Bolivia. Now a major tourism hub.

label: San Juan
sub: Birthplace of Domingo Faustino Sarmiento (1811–1888), liberal president and father of public education. The January 15, 1944 earthquake destroyed the city (10,000+ dead), triggering national relief efforts led by then-Colonel Perón — where he met Evita.

label: San Carlos de Bariloche
sub: Andean Patagonian resort city. Received Nazi war criminals post-WWII, including Erich Priebke and Klaus Barbie's associates. Site of important Mapuche territorial claims. Major Argentine tourism destination and chocolate capital.

label: Iguazú Falls
sub: UNESCO World Heritage Site on the Argentine-Brazilian border. Largest waterfall system in the world by average flow. Argentina controls 80% of the falls. Major ecotourism anchor generating significant foreign exchange revenue.

label: Mar del Plata
sub: Argentina's main Atlantic beach resort city; major fishing port and industrial hub. Summit of the Americas held here in 2005 where Chávez and Kirchner blocked the US-backed FTAA (Free Trade Area of the Americas) proposal.

label: Jujuy — Quebrada de Humahuaca
sub: UNESCO World Heritage Site (2003). The Quebrada de Humahuaca is a 10,000-year-old trade and cultural route along the Andes. Jujuy province, site of General Belgrano's 'Éxodo Jujeño' (1812) — mass civilian evacuation before Spanish royalist forces.

label: Río Gallegos
sub: Capital of Santa Cruz Province. Hometown and political base of Néstor Kirchner, who served as provincial governor (1991–2003) before becoming president. Key Patagonian oil and sheep-farming city.

label: Bahía Blanca
sub: Argentina's largest Atlantic deep-water port after Buenos Aires. Major petrochemical complex (PBB Polisur, Profertil). Key export hub for Patagonian wheat, sunflower oil, and energy products. Historically important military garrison city.

label: La Plata
sub: Capital of Buenos Aires Province, founded April 19, 1882 by Governor Dardo Rocha as a planned city after Buenos Aires was federalized. Home of UNLP, one of Argentina's largest public universities. Suffered intense Dirty War repression; site of 'La Noche de los Lápices' (Night of the Pencils, 1976) in which student activists were kidnapped and killed.

label: Trelew — 1972 Massacre
sub: On August 22, 1972, Argentine Navy forces executed 16 political prisoners at the Trelew Naval Air Base following a failed escape attempt by ERP and Montoneros guerrillas. The Trelew Massacre shocked the nation and intensified political violence leading to the 1976 coup. In 2012, a federal court recognized it as a crime against humanity and sentenced three officers.

label: Puerto Madryn
sub: Patagonian port established by Welsh settlers on July 28, 1865 as Y Wladfa — the Welsh Colony. The community sought to preserve Welsh language and culture outside British control. Today the main gateway to Peninsula Valdés UNESCO World Heritage Site (right whales, elephant seals, penguins). Welsh heritage, including Cymraeg language, persists in nearby Gaiman and Trelew.

label: Santiago del Estero
sub: Argentina's oldest continuously inhabited city, founded July 25, 1553 by Francisco de Aguirre — 27 years before Buenos Aires. Capital of the country's poorest province, historically marked by political clientelism and entrenched land inequality. The 1993 'Santiagueñazo' — in which protesters burned the provincial government palace and courthouse — was a harbinger of the 2001 national crisis.

label: Parque Nacional Los Glaciares
sub: Argentina's largest national park (726,927 ha), established May 11, 1937; UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1981. Home to the Perito Moreno Glacier — one of the world's few advancing glaciers and a major symbol of Argentine Patagonia. Lakes Argentino and Viedma are fed by the Southern Patagonian Ice Field. Major ecotourism driver and critical climate change monitoring site.

label: Resistencia
sub: Capital of Chaco Province in the northeastern Gran Chaco region. Home to large Qom (Toba), Moqoit, and Wichí indigenous communities facing persistent poverty and unresolved land rights disputes. Known as the 'city of sculptures' for its 700+ public artworks. Major center for cotton and quebracho timber processing. One of Argentina's most economically precarious provincial capitals.

label: Paraná
sub: Capital of Entre Ríos Province on the eastern bank of the Paraná River. Served as capital of the Argentine Confederation (1853–1861) under Justo José de Urquiza after Buenos Aires Province temporarily seceded. The 1853 constitutional framework that unified Argentina was built here. Important Paraná River commerce hub and gateway to the Mesopotamia region.

label: Corrientes
sub: Capital of Corrientes Province, founded April 3, 1588 on the Paraná River. Major theatre of the War of the Triple Alliance (1864–1870), in which Argentina, Brazil, and Uruguay devastated Paraguay — killing an estimated 60–90% of Paraguay's adult male population. The war was launched partly from Argentine territory, making Corrientes a strategic base. Major cultural hub of the Litoral region.
